text: In this video we show the formation of a continent ileostomy or a cock pouch. In the first video we show the formation done in a human cadaver. In the second movie we will show it being performed in a patient.

The surgeon plans the continent ileostomy pouch by dividing the small bowel into three segments: a 7 cm segment for the stoma, a 10 cm segment for the valve, and two 15 cm segments for the pouch. The pouch is opened on the anti-mesenteric side, and the back wall is closed using threo polysorb sutures. A peritonectomy is performed on the valvular mesentery to enhance adhesion. The 10 cm segment is intercepted to form a 5 cm valve using Ellis clamps and secured with a 60 mm GIA blue stapler, avoiding mesentery stapling to preserve blood supply. The anterior wall is closed with a running suture.

The surgeon reverts the pouch through the mesentery, aligning the mesentery neatly. A catheter is inserted through the stoma to perform a leak test, demonstrating the pouch's continence as no water escapes. The stoma is prepared and brought through, with the pouch being fixed to the abdominal wall using at least four sutures.

The surgeons plan the pouch by marking the small bowel with sutures. The small bowel, designated to become the pouch, is opened on the anti-mesenteric side using diathermy. The back wall of the pouch is closed with a continuous suture. A peritonectomy of the valvular mesentery is performed, followed by the interception of the valve, which is secured with bladeless GIA staplers. A catheter is placed to confirm easy passage into the pouch. The valve is fixed to the pouch to prevent de-interception, and the anterior wall of the pouch is closed. After eversion, the pouch is filled to check for leaks and confirm continence. Finally, the pouch is fixed to the abdominal wall, and the stoma is matured.
